service.description: Azure Storage Account appears across 60 normalized findings in 5 checklist families. 1 GA-ready families can anchor the baseline. 4 lower-confidence families broaden design coverage.
service.whatThisMeans: Start with the GA-ready families for this service, then widen into preview guidance only when the review question requires more depth.

notes: An official Azure Product Availability by Region offering could not be matched automatically for this service.

quality.label: GA · High confidence
quality.summary: Strong review accelerator. Quality score 91 with 78% metadata completeness and 100% severity coverage.

quality.label: Preview · Medium confidence
quality.summary: Use with architectural judgment. Preview content is valuable, but it should not be treated as equivalent to mature review baselines.

quality.label: Preview · Medium confidence
quality.summary: Use with architectural judgment. Preview content is valuable, but it should not be treated as equivalent to mature review baselines.

quality.label: Preview · Medium confidence
quality.summary: Use with architectural judgment. Preview content is valuable, but it should not be treated as equivalent to mature review baselines.

quality.label: Preview · Medium confidence
quality.summary: Use with architectural judgment. Preview content is valuable, but it should not be treated as equivalent to mature review baselines.

items.text: Classic Storage Accounts must be migrated to new Azure Resource Manager resources
items.description: Classic storage accounts will be fully retired on August 31, 2024. If you have classic storage accounts, start planning your migration now.

items.text: Ensure that storage accounts are zone or region redundant
items.description: Redundancy ensures storage accounts meet availability and durability targets amidst failures, weighing lower costs against higher availability. Locally redundant storage offers the least durability at the lowest cost.

items.text: Enable container soft delete for the storage account to recover a deleted container and its contents.
Enable secure transfer to storage accounts.
Avoid overly broad CORS policies
items.description: Storage supports CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing), i.e. an HTTP feature that enables web apps from a different domain to loosen the same-origin policy. When enabling CORS, keep the CorsRules to the least privilege.

items.text: Consider disabling storage account keys, so that only Microsoft Entra ID access (and user delegation SAS) is supported.
items.description: Storage account keys ('shared keys') have very little audit capabilities. While it can be monitored on who/when fetched a copy of the keys, once the keys are in the hands of multiple people, it is impossible to attribute usage to a specific user. Solely relying on Entra ID authentication makes it easier to tie storage access to a user.
items.graph: resources | where type == 'microsoft.storage/storageaccounts' | extend allowSharedKeyAccess = tostring(properties.allowSharedKeyAccess) | extend compliant = (isnotempty(allowSharedKeyAccess) and allowSharedKeyAccess == 'false') | distinct id, compliant

items.text: Consider immutable blobs
items.description: Consider 'legal hold' or 'time-based retention' policies for blobs, so that is is impossible to delete the blob, the container, or the storage account. Please note that 'impossible' actually means 'impossible'; once a storage account contains an immutable blob, the only way to 'get rid' of that storage account is by cancelling the Azure subscription.

items.text: Consider storing connection strings in Azure KeyVault (in scenarios where managed identities are not possible)
items.description: Ideally, your application should be using a managed identity to authenticate to Azure Storage. If that is not possible, consider having the storage credential (connection string, storage account key, SAS, service principal credential) in Azure KeyVault or an equivalent service.

items.text: Consider using Azure Monitor to audit control plane operations on the storage account
items.description: Use Activity Log data to identify 'when', 'who', 'what' and 'how' the security of your storage account is being viewed or changed (i.e. storage account keys, access policies, etc.).

items.text: Consider using private endpoints for Azure Storage
items.description: Azure Storage by default has a public IP address and is Internet-reachable. Private endpoints allow to securely expose Azure Storage only to those Azure Compute resources that need access, thus eliminating exposure to the public Internet
items.graph: resources | where type =~ 'Microsoft.Storage/StorageAccounts' | where isnull(properties.privateEndpointConnections) or properties.privateEndpointConnections[0].properties.provisioningState != ('Succeeded') or (isnull(properties.networkAcls) and properties.publicNetworkAccess == 'Enabled') | extend compliant = (isnotnull(properties.privateEndpointConnections) and properties.privateEndpointConnections[0].properties.provisioningState == 'Succeeded' and properties.publicNetworkAccess == 'Disabled') | distinct id, compliant

items.text: Consider whether public blob anonymous access is needed, or whether it can be disabled for certain storage accounts.
items.description: Anonymous access may present a security risk. We recommend that you disable anonymous access for optimal security. Disallowing anonymous access helps to prevent data breaches caused by undesired anonymous access.
items.graph: resources | where type == 'microsoft.storage/storageaccounts' | extend compliant = (properties.allowBlobPublicAccess == 'false') | distinct id, compliant

items.text: Determine how data at rest should be encrypted. Understand the thread model for data.
items.description: Data at rest is always encrypted server-side, and in addition might be encrypted client-side as well. Server-side encryption might happen using a platform-managed key (default) or customer-managed key. Client-side encryption might happen by either having the client supply an encryption/decryption key on a per-blob basis to Azure storage, or by completely handling encryption on the client-side. thus not relying on Azure Storage at all for confidentiality guarantees.

items.text: Enable 'soft delete' for containers
items.description: Soft delete for containers enables you to recover a container after it has been deleted, for example recover from an accidental delete operation.

items.text: Enable Microsoft Defender for all of your storage accounts
items.description: Leverage Microsoft Defender to learn about suspicious activity and misconfigurations.
items.graph: resources | where type =~ 'Microsoft.Storage/StorageAccounts' | project storageAccountId = id | join kind=leftouter (resourceContainers | where type == 'microsoft.security/pricings' | where name == 'StorageAccounts' | project resourceId = id, pricingTier = properties.pricingTier) on $left.storageAccountId == $right.resourceId | where isnull(pricingTier) or pricingTier != 'Standard' | extend compliant = false | distinct storageAccountId, compliant

items.text: Enable resource locks on storage accounts
items.description: Prevents accidental deletion of a storage account, by forcing the user to first remove the deletion lock, prior to deletion

items.text: Enforce the latest TLS version for a storage account
items.description: . Enforcing the latest TLS version will reject request from clients using the older version.
items.graph: resources | where type == 'microsoft.storage/storageaccounts' | extend compliant = (isnull(properties.minimumTlsVersion) == false and properties.minimumTlsVersion in ('TLS1_2', 'TLS1_3')) | distinct id, compliant

items.text: Leverage a storagev2 account type for better performance and reliability
Leverage GRS, ZRS or GZRS storage for the highest availability
items.graph: resources | where type =~ 'Microsoft.Storage/StorageAccounts' | extend compliant = (sku.name != 'Standard_LRS' and sku.name != 'Premium_LRS') | distinct id, compliant

items.text: Require HTTPS, i.e. disable port 80 on the storage account
items.description: Consider disabling unprotected HTTP/80 access to the storage account, so that all data transfers are encrypted, integrity protected, and the server is authenticated.
items.graph: resources | where type =~ 'Microsoft.Storage/StorageAccounts' | extend compliant = (properties.supportsHttpsTrafficOnly == false) | distinct id, compliant

items.text: SFTP: Limit the amount of 'local users' for SFTP access, and audit whether access is needed over time.
items.description: When accessing blob storage via SFTP using a 'local user account', the 'usual' RBAC controls do not apply. Blob access via NFS or REST might be more restrictive than SFTP access. Unfortunately, as of early 2023, local users are the only form of identity management that is currently supported for the SFTP endpoint

items.text: Strive for short validity periods for ad-hoc SAS
items.description: Use near-term expiration times on an ad hoc SAS service SAS or account SAS. In this way, even if a SAS is compromised, it's valid only for a short time. This practice is especially important if you cannot reference a stored access policy. Near-term expiration times also limit the amount of data that can be written to a blob by limiting the time available to upload to it.

items.text: Use Microsoft Entra ID tokens for blob access
items.description: Microsoft Entra ID tokens should be favored over shared access signatures, wherever possible

items.text: When enforcing HTTPS (disabling HTTP), check that you do not use custom domains (CNAME) for the storage account.
items.description: When configuring a custom domain (hostname) on a storage account, check whether you need TLS/HTTPS; if so, you might have to put Azure CDN in front of your storage account.

items.text: When using SAS, prefer 'user delegation SAS' over storage-account-key based SAS.
items.description: A user delegation SAS is secured with Azure Active Directory (Azure AD) credentials and also by the permissions specified for the SAS. A user delegation SAS is analogous to a service SAS in terms of its scope and function, but offers security benefits over the service SAS.

items.text: Enable Azure Private Link service for storage accounts
items.description: Leverage Azure Private Link Service for secure access to Azure Storage and services via Private Endpoint in your VNet. Eliminate the need for public IPs, ensuring data privacy. Enjoy granular access control for enhanced security.
items.graph:
// Azure Resource Graph Query
// This resource graph query will return all storage accounts that does not have a Private Endpoint Connection or where a private endpoint exists but public access is enabled
resources
| where type =~ "Microsoft.Storage/StorageAccounts"
| where isnull(properties.privateEndpointConnections) or properties.privateEndpointConnections[0].properties.provisioningState != ("Succeeded") or (isnull(properties.networkAcls) and properties.publicNetworkAccess == 'Enabled')
| extend param1 = strcat('Private Endpoint: ', iif(isnotnull(properties.privateEndpointConnections),split(properties.privateEndpointConnections[0].properties.privateEndpoint.id,'/')[8],'No Private Endpoint'))
| extend param2 = strcat('Access: ', iif(properties.publicNetworkAccess == 'Disabled', 'Public Access Disabled', iif(isnotnull(properties.networkAcls), 'NetworkACLs in place','Public Access Enabled')))
| project recommendationId = "dc55be60-6f8c-461e-a9d5-a3c7686ed94e", name, id, tags, param1, param2

items.text: Enable Soft Delete to protect your data
items.description: The soft delete option enables data recovery if mistakenly deleted, while the Lock feature prevents the accidental deletion of the storage account itself, ensuring additional security and data integrity measures.

items.text: Use premium performance block blob storage for high performance workloads
items.description: Use premium performance block blob storage instead of standard performance storage for workloads that require fast storage response times and/or high transaction rates.

items.text: Apply a narrow scope to a SAS
items.description: When creating a SAS, be as specific and restrictive as possible. Prefer a SAS for a single resource and operation over a SAS which gives much broader access.

items.text: Consider configuring an SAS expiration policy
items.description: A SAS expiration policy specifies a recommended interval over which the SAS is valid. SAS expiration policies apply to a service SAS or an account SAS. When a user generates service SAS or an account SAS with a validity interval that is larger than the recommended interval, they'll see a warning.

items.text: Consider configuring your application's source code repository to detect checked-in connection strings and storage account keys.
Consider linking SAS to a stored access policy
items.description: Stored access policies give you the option to revoke permissions for a service SAS without having to regenerate the storage account keys.

items.text: Consider scoping SAS to a specific client IP address, wherever possible
items.description: A SAS can include parameters on which client IP addresses or address ranges are authorized to request a resource using the SAS.

items.text: Consider the 'Azure security baseline for storage'
items.description: Apply guidance from the Microsoft cloud security benchmark related to Storage

items.text: Determine which/if client-side encryption should be used.
Determine which/if platform encryption should be used.
Disable 'soft delete' for blobs
items.description: Consider selectively disabling 'soft delete' for certain blob containers, for example if the application must ensure that deleted information is immediately deleted, e.g. for confidentiality, privacy or compliance reasons.

items.text: Disable 'soft delete' for containers
items.description: Consider selectively disabling 'soft delete' for certain blob containers, for example if the application must ensure that deleted information is immediately deleted, e.g. for confidentiality, privacy or compliance reasons.

items.text: Enable 'soft delete' for blobs
items.description: The soft-delete mechanism allows to recover accidentally deleted blobs.

items.text: Enable Soft Delete
Ensure older storage accounts are not using 'classic deployment model'
items.description: Newly created storage accounts are created using the ARM deployment model, so that RBAC, auditing etc. are all enabled. Ensure that there are no old storage accounts with classic deployment model in a subscription

items.text: For write operation after failover, use customer-Managed Failover
Least privilege in IaM permissions
items.description: When assigning a role to a user, group, or application, grant that security principal only those permissions that are necessary for them to perform their tasks. Limiting access to resources helps prevent both unintentional and malicious misuse of your data.

items.text: Limit shared access signature (SAS) tokens to HTTPS connections only
items.description: Requiring HTTPS when a client uses a SAS token to access blob data helps to minimize the risk of credential loss.

items.text: SFTP: The SFTP endpoint does not support POSIX-like ACLs.
Understand Microsoft-Managed Failover details
When using storage account keys, consider enabling a 'key expiration policy'
items.description: A key expiration policy enables you to set a reminder for the rotation of the account access keys. The reminder is displayed if the specified interval has elapsed and the keys have not yet been rotated.

items.text: Consider archiving tiers for less used data
Consider moving unused storage to lower tier, with customized rule - https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/storage/blobs/lifecycle-management-policy-configure
Consider using standard SSD rather than Premium or Ultra where possible
For storage accounts, make sure that the chosen tier is not adding up transaction charges (it might be cheaper to move to the next tier)
Storage accounts: check hot tier and/or GRS necessary
Choose the most appropriate data redundancy option for Azure Storage based on your requirements
items.description: Use Zone-redundant Storage (ZRS) in the primary region for scenarios that require high availability and for restricting replication to a particular country or region. For protection against regional disasters, use Geo-zone-redundant Storage (GZRS), which combines ZRS in the primary region with geo-replication to a secondary region?.

items.text: Consider upgrading legacy storage accounts to v2 storage accounts
items.description: General-purpose v2 accounts are recommended for most storage scenarios offering the latest features or the lowest per-gigabyte pricing. Legacy accounts like Standard general-purpose v1 and Blob Storage aren't advised by Microsoft but may fit specific scenarios.
items.graph:
// Azure Resource Graph Query
// Find all Azure Storage Accounts, that upgradeable to General purpose v2.
Resources
| where type =~ "Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts" and kind in~ ("Storage", "BlobStorage")
| extend
    param1 = strcat("AccountKind: ", case(kind =~ "Storage", "Storage (general purpose v1)", kind =~ "BlobStorage", "BlobStorage", kind)),
    param2 = strcat("Performance: ", sku.tier),
    param3 = strcat("Replication: ", sku.name)
| project recommendationId = "2ad78dec-5a4d-4a30-8fd1-8584335ad781", name, id, tags, param1, param2, param3

items.text: Enable point-in-time restore for GPv2 accounts to safeguard against data loss
items.description: Consider enabling point-in-time restore for standard general purpose v2 accounts with flat namespace to protect against accidental deletion or corruption by restoring block blob data to an earlier state.

items.text: Enable versioning for accidental modification and keep the number of versions below 1000
items.description: Consider enabling versioning for Azure Storage Accounts to recover from accidental modifications or deletions and manage blob operation latency. Microsoft advises maintaining fewer than 1000 versions per blob to optimize performance. Lifecycle management can help delete old versions automatically.

items.text: Monitor all blob storage accounts
items.description: For critical applications and business processes relying on Azure, monitoring and alerts are crucial. Resource logs are only stored after creating a diagnostic setting to route logs to specified locations, requiring selection of log categories to collect.

items.text: Consider checking uploaded data, after clients used a SAS to upload a file.
items.description: A SAS cannot constrain how much data a client uploads; given the pricing model of amount of storage over time, it might make sense to validate whether clients uploaded maliciously large contents.

items.text: Apply a Delete lock to prevent accidental or malicious deletion of storage accounts
items.description: Assigning a Delete lock to your storage account helps protect the availability of your data, minimizing the risk of disruptions to your business operations.

items.text: Enable soft delete for blobs
items.description: Blob soft delete protects an individual blob and its versions, snapshots, and metadata from accidental deletes or overwrites by maintaining the deleted data in the system for a specified period of time.

items.text: Enable soft delete for Storage Account Containers
items.description: Container soft delete protects your data from being accidentally deleted by maintaining the deleted data in the system for a specified period of time.

notes: An official Azure Product Availability by Region offering could not be matched automatically for this service.
